title: Life Between Papers
subtitle: Every document tells the truth. None tells the whole truth
caption: Everyone lives between papers. Adoption only makes the blank middle visible: one record sealed, one certificate issued, and a life the adoptee must witness for himself.
artworkAlt: A government triplicate form on a dark desk, headed Certificate of Extinguishment, with a red SEALED stamp
body:
Somerset House will not simply hand you your own beginning. First, there’s a room and a counsellor the law insists must sit across from me before I am permitted to read the sentence that says I was born.

She’s kind; she’s done this before. That is somehow the worst of it, that there’s a procedure for this, a chair worn smooth by other people arriving to be told, gently, on the record, that they exist. Then there’s a fee. You pay, in the end, to be introduced to yourself.
fullBody:
Every document tells the truth. None tells the whole truth.

Somerset House will not simply hand you your own beginning. First, there’s a room and a counsellor the law insists must sit across from me before I am permitted to read the sentence that says I was born.

She’s kind; she’s done this before. That is somehow the worst of it, that there’s a procedure for this, a chair worn smooth by other people arriving to be told, gently, on the record, that they exist. Then there’s a fee. You pay, in the end, to be introduced to yourself.

And then, from somewhere behind glass and granite, out of the cold steel and the microfiche and the labyrinth of cabinets, they bring it up into the light: a small sheet, heavier than I expect, blue-black ink on paper the colour of baby sick. My name is on it. Not the name I answer to. The first one. Jonathan Richard Jordan. Seven May, 1967. Eight-fifty in the morning, an upstairs room in a two-up two-down terraced house on Union Street, Southport, next door to a pub. Ten minutes later, the same room was used again, unexpectedly. My identical twin brother arriving behind me into the same small square of the world.

Two beginnings, in one room, before breakfast.

Two names would be sealed that decade, not one. We were carried out of that first story together, under a single new surname, our given names left, thankfully, intact. Someone chose to keep those, my mother. I have since learned how rare that kindness was.

We were carried out together but we haven’t stayed that way. The years the state declined to record did their own quiet work on us, and drew us to opposite edges of the one story we were meant to share. We barely speak now, the only other living person who carries the exact shape of this erasure, and the one witness I cannot ask.

I keep coming back to one detail. Not the name. The margin. There, in a hand that was never mine, entered by someone whose face I will never see, is a single word.

Adopted.

Not a sentence. Not an explanation. One word, scrawled in the only place the state left for it, on the only paper that ever proved I happened.

I had gone looking for a record. What I found was a verdict.

---

Here is the mechanism. When a child is adopted in the UK, the original entry, the true one, made in the hours after the birth, is sealed. The Adoption Order comes down, the Registrar General enters a new record in a separate book, the Adopted Children Register, and issues from it a fresh certificate. That certificate is the one you grew up inside. It goes to the school, the passport office, and the registrar at your wedding. It works. It is, in every legal sense, sound.

It is also, in the places that matter most, invented.

The date survives, seven May, the one true thing carried across the border. But the room doesn’t travel with it. The house on Union Street simply falls off the edge of the document. New names arrive by order of the court. A whole prior history isn’t so much erased as withdrawn from ordinary issue, the way a form is retired: still filed somewhere, but no longer given out.

So the sequence is this. First, an administrative death. Quiet enough that almost nobody notices. The sealing of the original record. A death with no body and no funeral, perhaps the only one you are expected to outlive.

Then comes a birth. Legally impeccable. Personally fictional. Death, then birth. Both were performed without ceremony by people simply doing their jobs. I was raised to believe a birth certificate was a fact about a person. It took me most of my life to understand it is a decision made about a person, usually while they are far too young to be consulted.

True and legal were never the same promise. They were handed to me in the same envelope, wearing the same ink, and I assumed, as everyone assumes, that they were one thing. They are not. I learned the difference at a counter, holding two versions of myself, neither of which had asked my permission. Step back far enough from my one small sheet of paper, and a larger shape appears, and it is not only mine.

---

Look at what the state actually certifies. It stamps the entrance. It will, one day, stamp the exit. Birth and death: the two hinges of a life, witnessed, numbered, filed. And between them, the entire thing, the only part any of us actually live: nothing.

There is no form for the middle. No registrar issues a certificate for the years themselves. No office keeps a record that you were here in any sense richer than arrived and departed. For most people, this absence is invisible and harmlessly so. The middle gets witnessed anyway, informally, unpaid. A mother says you have your grandfather's hands. A face in a photograph rhymes with your own. Family and memory do the registrar's work quietly, for free, and nobody notices there was a job to be done at all, because it was always already done.

That is the design. The state leaves the middle blank on purpose and trusts the family to fill it. It isn’t an oversight. It’s the quiet assumption underneath the whole system: that somebody, somewhere, unofficially, is keeping the record of your life.

Everyone lives between papers. That’s not the adopted person's peculiar tragedy; it’s the ordinary human condition, only made visible. The difference is this: most people inherit their witnesses and never have to think about it. Some of us are handed the blank forms directly.

Take away the informal registrars. Seal the first record. Withhold the resemblances. Remove the face in the photograph that was supposed to rhyme with yours. Do all of that before the child has language to notice it is being done, and the blank middle the state left for the family to fill has no one left to fill it.

The work doesn't disappear. Someone still has to keep the record.

For most people, family does it without ever thinking about it. Stories are repeated. Resemblances are noticed. Someone remembers the house, the holiday, the argument, the laugh. Your life is witnessed back to you until belonging becomes ordinary.

But when those witnesses are missing, the work remains. Someone still has to say, "*This life happened."*

Eventually, I realised that someone was me.

---

I’ve spent years doing that work without knowing it was what I was doing. Every broken relationship, each line of cocaine, and every unmet potential, it was all, I see now, the same act: testimony doing a registrar's job. Sworn, unwitnessed, unpaid, and necessary, the only method left to a person whose record the authorities refused to keep.

Testimony has been issuing my certificates all along. It simply never occurred to me to call it that.

So let me call it that now. And let me say it to the two of you, Ella and Imogen, because you are the reason the naming finally matters.

No registrar was ever going to give me the document I actually needed. Not the one that says I was born; I have that, in two versions, and neither told the truth I was after. I mean the other one, the one that certifies not the entrance and not the exit but the middle: that the living happened, that it was mine, that the years between the two stamps were real and were spent and counted for something.

There is no office for that. I checked. So I did the only thing left to a person who has learned that authorship outranks any seal.

I wrote it myself.

This book is that document. Not a metaphor for it. The thing itself.

The registrar certifies that a birth occurred. I can certify what it was for. And a certificate is nothing until it is witnessed, which is why this one is addressed to you two, deliberately, because yours are the only signatures I have ever actually wanted on anything.

They recorded my birth. They recorded my adoption. The life that followed was left for someone else to witness.

Someone else did.
beforeReading: This essay begins at a counter in Somerset House and follows the paperwork of adoption outward into a larger claim: the state certifies only birth and death, and leaves the living years to be witnessed by someone. When that someone is missing, testimony has to do a registrar's job.
originTitle: It began with a word in the margin.
origin: I kept returning to the margin of my original birth record, and to the strangeness of holding two versions of myself: one sealed, one issued. The revised essay follows that sequence more closely into twinship, estrangement, and the blank middle everyone lives in, only some of us without inherited witnesses.
meaningTitle: The life between the stamps still has to be witnessed.
meaning: Writing this clarified that the book, addressed to my daughters, is the record of the middle: not the entrance and not the exit, but that the living happened and counted for something. Everyone lives between papers; authorship is how some of us keep the form the state left blank.
seoTitle: Life Between Papers | Adoption, Records, and the Life Between the Stamps
seoDescription: Every document tells the truth. None tells the whole truth. Jonathan Lyon on sealed and issued birth records, the blank middle no office certifies, and writing a life for his daughters. Companion to an interactive certificate artefact.

recordCard.kicker: Filed nowhere. In triplicate. Retain no copy but this one.
recordCard.title: Certificate of a Life

fields.label: Bearer
fields.value: the one who kept the record himself

fields.label: Born
fields.value: 7 May 1967, and every day since, on purpose

fields.label: Place
fields.value: an upstairs room; and then everywhere; and then, at last, here, with you

fields.label: Evidence submitted
fields.value: this. All of it. Every page.

fields.label: Issued by
fields.value: the bearer, at his own desk, unpaid, under no authority but the years themselves

fields.label: Witnessed
fields.value: Ella, and Imogen, on the day they sign

recordCard.footer: No stamp is available for this. Affix nothing. It counts anyway.

recognitionTitle: The paperwork was recognised as a shared inheritance.
recognitionIntro: Responses from readers when this testimony was first shared.
